title: Fidelity bounds for spin-dependent kicks with pulsed lasers
authors: C. Sagaseta, H. Liu, V. D. Vaidya, C. R. Viteri, J. J. García-Ripoll, E. Torrontegui
abstract: Excitation of trapped-ion hyperfine qubits with fast optical Raman pulses enables faster-than-trap-period entangling gates with qubits of long coherence time for practical quantum computation. Achieving high-fidelity fast two-qubit gates requires high-quality spin-dependent kicks (SDKs), which form their fundamental building blocks. Here, we characterize the control parameters (including Raman frequency difference, pulse arrival times, Lamb--Dicke parameter, temperature, pulse width, and SDK time) that maximize the performance of single-ion SDKs for protocols compatible with performed experiments involving a small number of fast pulses. We demonstrate through analytical methods and numerical simulations that, within the model commonly used for infidelity optimization, finite pulse duration is the dominant source of error, exceeding the contribution of secular motion by orders of magnitude for nanosecond-scale SDKs. Low infidelities -- below $10^{-3}$ for schemes with $\gtrsim10$ fixed-amplitude, equispaced, picosecond pulses -- are achievable in SDK times on the order of nanoseconds. These results provide quantitative design rules for achieving competitive SDK fidelities with current pulsed-laser technology, laying the foundation for sub-microsecond trapped-ion quantum entangling operations.
summary: This paper rigorously analyzes the fidelity of Spin-Dependent Kicks (SDKs) used in trapped-ion quantum computing. By moving beyond idealized assumptions, the authors determine that the primary source of error limiting gate fidelity is the finite duration of the laser pulses, not the ion's residual motion. The results provide actionable guidelines for achieving high-fidelity, sub-microsecond quantum operations.
main_problem: The paper aims to characterize the control parameters required to maximize the fidelity of single-ion Spin-Dependent Kicks (SDKs) for implementing fast, high-fidelity two-qubit gates in trapped-ion quantum computing.
main_result: Finite pulse duration is found to be the dominant source of error, exceeding the contribution of secular motion by orders of magnitude for nanosecond-scale SDKs. Low infidelities below $10^{-3}$ are achievable with picosecond pulses.
method: The analysis uses analytical methods and numerical simulations to model the evolution operator under pulsed Raman laser interactions, systematically relaxing approximations used in previous studies.
model_or_system: The physical system is trapped ions with qubits encoded in hyperfine levels. The interaction is governed by a time-dependent Hamiltonian involving the trap frequency, qubit frequency, and pulsed laser fields, modeled as a train of short pulses.
key_observables: Fidelity of the SDK, state-averaged process fidelity ($ ar{F}$), and state-averaged infidelity ($ ar{I}$).
important_parameters: Raman frequency difference ($\delta$), pulse arrival times ($t_n$), Lamb–Dicke parameter ($\eta$), pulse width ($	au$), and total SDK time ($T_{SDK}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis moves beyond previous approximations by incorporating finite pulse width and considering the effects of ion secular motion, though the error from motion is found to be negligible compared to pulse duration errors.
figures_summary: Figures schematically illustrate the pulsed Raman SDK structure and energy level diagrams. Other figures compare infidelity vs. pulse number for different pulse widths, and show phase space pictures of SDK evolution.
paper_structure: The paper builds by first defining the ideal SDK operator, then analyzing the protocol by sequentially relaxing approximations (from large $N$ to finite $	au$ and motion), culminating in quantitative design rules based on the dominant error source.
why_interesting: This work provides crucial, quantitative design rules for implementing fundamental quantum gates (SDKs) in trapped ions by precisely quantifying the limitations imposed by realistic pulse laser technology.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of the pulsed Raman SDK. Two counter-propagating beams in the lin ⊥lin polarization configuration interact with a single ion (grey ball) confined in a harmonic potential (grey parabola) with characteristic length x0, that corresponds to the zero-point spread of the motional wavepacket of the ion. The quantization axis is defined by the magnetic field B = Bx, with x the unit vector along x. A SDK is generated by two pulse trains W(t) of duration TSDK composed of pulses A(t) of duration τ separated by equispaced intervals trep. (a) Phase space picture of a 5-pulse SDK with (path A) and without (path B) taking into account the secular motion of the ion, assuming equal-strength momentum kicks p = η/N (in units of ℏ/x0) depicted as black arrows.
